text: According to Shah and Burke (2005) and Richardson (2007), a labour shortage occurs when there is insufficient supply of workers willing to work under current market conditions, particularly prevailing wages. Factors causing shortages include lags in wage adjustments, labour supply responsiveness, and insufficient labour market information. Thus, methodologies for identifying shortages should consider factors like employment, wages, working hours, and vacancies. The top-down methodology utilised multiple data sources, including quantity indicators (employment levels, vacancies) and price indicators (earnings, wage premiums), applying specific thresholds to identify shortages. It then adopted a "traffic light" approach, whereby the final determination of a shortage was made by considering evidence from a certain percentage of indicators, rather than relying on evidence from a single indicator. 25 26

Using a benchmark period to set threshold values reduces the risk of incorrect inclusion and provides three (3) additional benefits (MAC 2010): • First, as the economy evolves, the indicators of labour market shortages also change. This means that fewer occupations are identified as being in shortage during periods of weak economic growth. • Second, indicators change along with skill shortages rather than other types of shortages that are more stable over time. • Finally, it eliminates the requirement to reevaluate the threshold values for each assessment period. This means that the suitability of using a p50+50% or p75 threshold need only be assessed for the benchmark period. MyCOL 2024/2025 used 2022 as the benchmark period. Figure 3 shows that 2022 recorded the highest annual gross domestic product (GDP) growth (8.7%) with an unemployment rate of 3.9% as compared to 2011 and 2022. The economic growth was higher in 2022 than in 2014. This qualifies 2022 to be selected as the benchmark period for this analysis. 31 32

The Regional Corridor Development Authority (RECODA) faces key challenges in Sarawak, including manpower shortages due to high industry demand and a smaller working-age population compared to Peninsular Malaysia, as well as a skills deficiency in Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) and STEM skills. Worker retention is also a concern, as many leave for higher-paying jobs. Although RECODA promotes TVET and short courses to equip workers with real-world skills, implementation is hindered by limited incentives. To address these issues, RECODA supports human capital development under the Sarawak Corridor of Renewable Energy (SCORE), focusing on job training, community empowerment, and infrastructure to boost local livelihoods. Under RECODA, several programmes target the B40 group in Sarawak to improve employability and skills across various industries. In Agriculture, courses such as Fresh Bunch Fruit Harvesting and Collection Management and Plantation Worker Skills Courses provide essential training in agriculture and plantation management. Industrial skills programmes include the Blaster & Painter Level 1 (PCT), Wireman Second Grade, and Oil & Gas Construction Crew training, focusing on technical skills for specific industries. Service industry skills training, like Frontline Customer Service, enhances communication and customer handling abilities. Additionally, the Communication and Digital Marketing Upskilling programme focuses on enhancing digital and marketing skills, providing participants with cutting-edge tools for social media and audience engagement. These programmes aim to boost employability for the B40 group across sectors such as agriculture, industry, services, and digital communication. 65 66

Note on TOP-DOWN DATA INDICATORS: The indicators are identified by looking for data that have occupational information which could point to evidence of shortage. A threshold is then set to determine the matrix of what would be considered in shortage or not. Indicators Shortage Rationale 1. Working hours growth (1 year) An increase in the median number of hours worked per week may suggest that rising labour demand is being met through longer working hours among the existing workforce. 2. Working hours growth (3 years) 3. Employment growth (1 year) An increase in the number of employees is often indicative of increasing demand for that occupation. 4. Employment growth (3 years) 5. Wage premium growth (1 year) An increase in wages often reflects rising demand for labour in that occupation. 6. Wage premium growth (3 years) 7. Education level decrease (1 year) Lowering education requirements can signal labour shortages, as employers adjust hiring criteria to secure workers for hard-to-fill positions. 8. Education level decrease (3 years) 9. Number of vacancies A high number of vacancies typically indicate increasing demand for labour that exceeds current supply. This indicator follows the same rationale as the vacancy measure, but it 10. Vacancy rate (% of employment) accounts for employment size within each occupation. Shifts in the education level can indicate employer adjustments in hiring 11. Falling education requirements in job postings standards to address hard-to-fill vacancies. 12. Degree of persistence in occupational shortage Repeated listing in the MyCOL over successive years suggests that it has been persistently difficult to fill. This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Chief Executive Officer, Chief Operating Officer, Executive Director, Company Director, Managing Director, Enterprise Director, Managing-Director of Organisation, Manager, General Manager, Operations Manager, Director, Administrator, Technical Manager, Head of Operation, Assistant Manager, and Technical Director. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, engineering technology, and personal skills. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, problem solving, and initiative and innovation. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in business operations and financial activities skills, operating procedures and processes skills, and communication and collaboration skills. The top technology tools nominated were business intelligence and decision support software, customer relationship management (CRM) software, and project management software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between 3-4 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and increase employees’

The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and increase employees’ training. v. In the horticulture sector, stakeholders expressed significant challenges in finding candidates with strong leadership skills. They highlighted the difficulty in recruiting individuals capable of managing diverse teams, driving strategic initiatives, and effectively navigating complex regulatory environments. 105 106

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Finance Manager, Account Manager, Audit Manager, Finance Director, Chief Financial Controller, Risk Manager, Head of Procurement, Procurement Manager, Risk Modelling Manager, and Actuarial Valuation Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of the vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, literacy and numeracy, and generic programmes and qualifications not further defined. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are numeracy, planning and organising, and problem solving. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in business operations and financial activities skills, safety and hazard management skills, and human resources skills. The top technology tools nominated were accounting software, financial analysis software, and enterprise resource planning ERP software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is harder to fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack relevant job experience, and expected compensation is beyond the market rate. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, raise wages, and increase employees’ training. v. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors

To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, raise wages, and increase employees’ training. v. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors highlighted significant challenges in recruiting and retaining finance managers and other key roles. In the insurance industry, stiff competition from countries like Singapore and the UK has made it increasingly difficult to retain trained actuaries, with consulting firms offering highly attractive packages that further drain the talent pool. Additionally, there is a limited supply of candidates with relevant industry experience and expertise in the local market. In the banking sector, both finance and risk management were identified as niche skill sets that are in high demand but difficult to find, exacerbating the sector’s struggle to fill these specialised positions. 109 110

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Chief Human Resources Officer, Human Resource Manager, Training Manager, Occupational Safety and Health Manager, Occupational Safety Manager, Health, Safety and Environment, and Quality Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are ranging from junior to senior level, but predominantly at the junior level, equivalent to 1-3 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, generic programmes and qualifications not further defined, and basic programmes and qualifications. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, oral communication, and initiative and innovation. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in safety and hazard management skills, communication and collaboration skills, and environmental management skills. The top technology tools nominated were audio/video conferencing software, human resources software, and database management software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications and relevant job experience, while post-pandemic cost-cutting prevents firms from offering market- rate salaries. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to increase employees’ training, raise wages, and expand local recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the horticulture sector expressed concerns about attracting qualified

applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications and relevant job experience, while post-pandemic cost-cutting prevents firms from offering market- rate salaries. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to increase employees’ training, raise wages, and expand local recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the horticulture sector expressed concerns about attracting qualified human resource managers, noting that the geographic location of many horticultural operations can be a significant barrier. Potential candidates often prefer roles in urban centres, making it challenging to attract talent to more rural or remote areas where horticulture businesses are typically based. This geographic factor limits the pool of available candidates for key HR roles in the sector. 113 114

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Strategic Planning Manager, Corporate Planning Manager, Programme Manager, and Regulatory Affairs Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the entry level, equivalent to fresh graduate. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack the required technical skills, and relevant job experience. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts and expand international recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the life sciences sector raised concerns about the lack of specialised education for policy and planning managers, particularly in regulatory affairs. looking to expand local recruitment efforts and expand international recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the life sciences sector raised concerns about the lack of specialised education for policy and planning managers, particularly in regulatory affairs. Currently, no major directly focuses on regulatory affairs, leaving the industry to rely on employees with backgrounds in pharmacology and pharmacy. These professionals often have knowledge of the Medical Devices Directive (MDD) and Medical Device Regulation (MDR) systems, but there remains a gap in formal training specific to the regulatory frameworks required for the sector. 117 118

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Production Manager, Production Planner, Production Controller, Business Development Manager, Compliance Manager, Supply Chain Manager, Business and Product Development Manager, Sustainability Manager, Project Manager, and Assistant Project Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, engineering, manufacturing and construction not further defined, and manufacturing and processing. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, initiative and innovation, and oral communication. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in communication and collaboration skills, business operations and financial activities skills, and operating procedures and processes skills. The top technology tools nominated were project management software, computer aided manufacturing, and industrial control and automation software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, majority of the firms mentioned that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between 5-6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the required technical skills. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, increase employees’ training, and raise wages. v. Stakeholders from the insurance

main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the required technical skills. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, increase employees’ training, and raise wages. v. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors highlighted significant challenges in filling business services manager roles, particularly in product development within the life insurance industry. These positions are highly specialised, especially for candidates with a comprehensive understanding of distribution channels, and the demand far exceeds the available talent pool. In the insurance sector, attracting younger talent is becoming increasingly difficult, as millennials and Generation Z often prefer career paths that offer more flexibility and stability over traditional sales roles. At the leadership level, many skilled professionals are seeking overseas or regional roles to gain broader exposure and benefit from more rewarding compensation packages. The fast-paced nature of the role, driven by evolving technology and societal trends, requires incumbents to stay continuously updated and creatively adapt to close deals. However, the sales profession remains stigmatised, with many perceiving it as pushy and unprofessional, further deterring top talent. Additionally, the pool of candidates with specialised financial, insurance, or Syariah expertise is limited. In the banking sector, niche skill sets remain scarce, with too few applicants available to meet demand for key roles, such as finance and risk management. 121 122

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Quality Control Manager and Quality Assurance Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for all vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in manufacturing and processing, biological and related sciences, and engineering, manufacturing and construction not further defined. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, initiative and innovation, and problem solving. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in business operations and financial activities skills, production processes and machinery skills, and communication and collaboration skills. The top technology tools nominated were quantitative schedule and risk analysis software, regulatory compliance software, and computer aided manufacturing. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, majority of the firms mentioned that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack other required skills. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, and establish or expand partnerships with education or training providers focused on recruitment of graduates. v. overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and outsource this job function. v. Stakeholders from the timber, horticulture, agribusiness, and livestock sectors raised significant concerns about the challenges in filling agricultural, forestry, and livestock production manager roles. In the timber sector, training workers to become plantation managers takes a considerable amount of time, with most candidates lacking the necessary technical skills. In horticulture, the role requires a unique combination of expertise in both environmental sustainability and business management, making it difficult to find candidates with the right skills and experience. Similarly, in agribusiness, agrofood processing, and livestock, there is a noticeable shortage of local candidates with the necessary skills. These positions require extensive hands-on experience in farm operations, which is often difficult to find in the domestic workforce. 133 134

The Malaysia Logistic Supply Chain Association (MLSCA) and the Federation of Malaysian Freight Forwarders (FMFF) stressed the vital role of Supply, Distribution, and Related Managers in ensuring seamless supply chain operations. These professionals are tasked with meeting customer expectations, managing costs effectively, and supporting global trade and sustainability initiatives. This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Mathematician (Applied Mathematics), Actuary, Pricing Actuarie, Actuarial Executive, and Chief Actuary. ii. Stakeholders from the insurance sector highlighted several key challenges in recruiting mathematicians, actuaries, and statisticians, particularly in relation to highly specialised roles such as those required for actuarial science and International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS 17) positions. The market is characterised by a limited talent pool, with scarce skills and intense competition for qualified candidates. Actuarial roles demand a unique blend of expertise in mathematics, statistics, and finance, and the rigorous qualification process, which includes passing multiple exams from professional bodies like the Society of Actuaries (SOA) or the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries (IFoA), can deter potential candidates or delay their entry into the workforce. Furthermore, the growing importance of risk management and data-driven decision-making has increased demand for actuaries not only within the insurance industry but also in consulting, finance, and other sectors, leading to greater competition for talent. Local expertise is also in short supply, with Malaysia competing against regional markets like Singapore and Hong Kong for the same pool of talent, particularly in reinsurance. These factors, combined with the scarcity of experienced professionals, have made it increasingly difficult to find candidates with the necessary skills, technical knowledge, and experience to fill these critical roles. iii. Based on the input from the consultation, the banking sector primarily seeks mathematicians, actuaries, and statisticians with at least 3-5 years of experience for most roles, though demand increases for more senior positions requiring 8 years or more. Leadership roles, especially in higher-tier positions, typically call for 10 to 15 years of experience, reflecting the need for deep expertise and proven strategic capabilities in the field. iv. To address the shortage of

experience for most roles, though demand increases for more senior positions requiring 8 years or more. Leadership roles, especially in higher-tier positions, typically call for 10 to 15 years of experience, reflecting the need for deep expertise and proven strategic capabilities in the field. iv. To address the shortage of mathematicians, actuaries, and statisticians in the banking sector, several measures have been implemented. These include encouraging professionals to continue pursuing and completing their actuarial qualifications through examination support and benefits. Industry workshops are organised to promote knowledge sharing and ensure that professionals stay informed about market trends. Fresh graduates are also recruited and groomed for future roles, with a focus on developing talent from within the organisation. Additionally, initiatives such as clear career development paths, education assistance programmes, actuarial study support, and attractive salary and benefits packages are in place to motivate and retain talent. v. The Actuarial Society of Malaysia (ASM) highlighted a significant shortage of experienced actuaries, driven by the rigorous nature of actuarial exams and the requirement for extensive on-the-job experience. This talent gap is further aggravated by the competition from other Asian regions, which are actively attracting skilled professionals. Actuaries are essential for managing complex financial risks, particularly in the expanding insurance and takaful sectors, as well as meeting the increasing demand for pension and retirement planning solutions. Addressing this shortage is critical to supporting the industry's growth and stability. 153 154

majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Stakeholders from the timber, petrochemical, machinery, and electronics sectors raised significant concerns regarding the recruitment of mechanical engineers. In the timber manufacturing sector, finding qualified candidates is challenging, and even when they are available, many are deterred by the long working hours and demanding work environments. Additionally, candidates are increasingly demanding higher wages due to rising inflation and the weakening Ringgit. The petrochemical industry also faces a shortage of candidates with essential skills in maintenance, troubleshooting, and safety. In the machinery and equipment sector, young engineers tend to favour working with software over hands-on plant work, and many are drawn to higher-paying roles in semiconductor manufacturing. Competing with multinational corporations (MNCs) in terms of pay remains a significant challenge. The electrical and electronics (E&E) sector reports that many candidates do not meet the necessary skill requirements, while Generation Z workers are increasingly prioritising work-life balance. Similarly, the life sciences sector struggles to find talent with experience in medical devices, further contributing to the skills gap across these industries. vi. Malaysia’s aerospace, pharmaceutical, and medical device sectors are already key contributors to the nation’s industrial landscape, each attracting major global players and

increasingly prioritising work-life balance. Similarly, the life sciences sector struggles to find talent with experience in medical devices, further contributing to the skills gap across these industries. vi. Malaysia’s aerospace, pharmaceutical, and medical device sectors are already key contributors to the nation’s industrial landscape, each attracting major global players and supporting significant technological advancement. The aerospace sector, home to companies manufacturing critical components such as wings, avionics, and drones, is set for substantial growth, with revenues projected to reach RM55 billion by 2030. Similarly, the pharmaceutical and medical device industries play an essential role in healthcare, driven by rising demand for innovative products like minimally invasive medical technologies and biologics. Both sectors are expected to see marked expansion, supporting Malaysia’s ambitions for a high- value, innovation-driven economy. The NIMP 2030 prioritises high value-added opportunities in aerospace, pharmaceutical, and medical device sectors, recognising their potential to drive Malaysia’s industrial and economic growth. In aerospace, the Plan focuses on advancing capabilities in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), system integration, and space technology, with aerospace engineers playing a pivotal role in leading innovation, product development, and advanced system design, particularly for space and defense applications. In the pharmaceutical and medical device sectors, the NIMP 2030 promotes the development of high-value products, including halal- certified medicines, biologics, and cutting-edge medical devices. Pharmaceutical and medical device engineers are essential to advancing R&D, ensuring compliance with international standards, and driving the creation of competitive, globally relevant technologies. 165 166

The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Electronics Engineer, Computer Applications Engineer, Computer Engineer, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Design Engineer, and Wintel Engineer (Electronic). ii. Vacancies within this occupation group are in junior level experience category, which is equivalent to 1-3 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for all vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in engineering and engineering trades and engineering technology. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are digital engagement and initiative and innovation. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in digital technologies and electronics skills and communication and collaboration skills. The top technology tools nominated were computer aided manufacturing, technical design and modelling software (2D and 3D), and statistical and data analysis software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, all the firms mentioned that this occupation is harder to fill as compared to a year ago. The average time it takes to fill these vacancies is more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack relevant job experience, while post-pandemic cost- cutting prevents firms from offering market-rate salaries. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand international recruitment efforts, increase employees’ training, and hire less well-qualified applicants. v. Stakeholders from the banking, machinery, and electronics sectors highlighted several challenges in recruiting electronics engineers. In the banking sector, electronic engineering skills are considered a niche, with few candidates possessing the specialised expertise required. The machinery and equipment sector

recruitment efforts, increase employees’ training, and hire less well-qualified applicants. v. Stakeholders from the banking, machinery, and electronics sectors highlighted several challenges in recruiting electronics engineers. In the banking sector, electronic engineering skills are considered a niche, with few candidates possessing the specialised expertise required. The machinery and equipment sector faces similar issues, with many young engineers preferring to work with software rather than addressing plant operations directly. Additionally, many talents are drawn to semiconductor manufacturing due to its higher pay, making it difficult for the sector to compete with multinational corporations (MNCs) in terms of salaries. In the electrical and electronics (E&E) sector, candidates often lack the necessary skill sets to become engineers, and Gen-Z workers are increasingly focused on achieving a strong work-life balance, further complicating recruitment efforts. vi. Malaysia is already a global hub for E&E manufacturing, particularly in assembly, testing, and packaging. The semiconductor sector forms about 60% of total E&E exports, and the New Industrial Master Plan 2030 (NIMP 2030) aims to deepen Malaysia’s involvement in front-end activities like integrated circuit (IC) design, which has higher value-added potential. NIMP 2030 focuses on creating global IC design champions in areas such as electric vehicles (EV), renewable energy (RE), and artificial intelligence (AI). As Malaysia transitions from being an assembly hub to an innovation-driven economy, the need for IC design engineers becomes pivotal in driving these sectors. Wafer fabrication is another crucial element in semiconductor manufacturing. Malaysia aims to expand its capabilities beyond the current focus on packaging and testing. This step is essential to increase its control over the entire semiconductor value chain. The NIMP 2030 has set a strategy to attract global leaders in wafer fabrication and establish mid-tier wafer fabrication facilities domestically. Wafer fabrication engineers are needed to develop, operate, and maintain these facilities,

packaging and testing. This step is essential to increase its control over the entire semiconductor value chain. The NIMP 2030 has set a strategy to attract global leaders in wafer fabrication and establish mid-tier wafer fabrication facilities domestically. Wafer fabrication engineers are needed to develop, operate, and maintain these facilities, which are expected to drive Malaysia’s participation in global semiconductor production. 185 186

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. This occupation did not receive any nominations from either the bottom-up CfE survey or the consultation session. However, it has been included in the MyCOL following validation with stakeholders. ii. The Malaysia Digital Economy Corporation (MDEC) has highlighted the high demand for graphic and multimedia designers, driven by the growing reliance on visual content across digital platforms and marketing channels. As businesses and organisations continue to prioritise digital engagement, the need for skilled designers to create compelling and effective visual content has surged. These professionals are essential in shaping brand identity, enhancing user experience, and driving successful marketing strategies in today’s digital economy. The Media Specialists Association (MSA) has pointed out the low interest in pursuing careers or courses in graphic and multimedia design, despite the growing demand for these skills in the digital and creative industries. This lack of interest has resulted in a talent shortage, making it increasingly difficult for businesses to find qualified professionals in this field. As visual content becomes more critical across digital platforms, addressing this gap is essential to meet industry needs and support the expanding creative economy. 189 190

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Marine Engineer, Ship Construction Engineer, Naval Architect, Marine Architect, and Chief Engineer (Marine). ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are ranging from junior to senior level, but predominantly at the middle level, equivalent to 3-5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in engineering, manufacturing and construction not further defined, literacy and numeracy, and engineering and engineering trades. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are initiative and innovation, digital engagement, and planning and organising. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in communication and collaboration skills and cleaning and maintenance skills. The top technology tools nominated were technical design and modelling software (2D and 3D), aviation and marine communication software, and computer aided manufacturing. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is harder to fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack relevant job experience, and the necessary credentials or qualifications. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and hire temporary or contract workers. v. The Association of Marine Industries of Malaysia (AMIM) and the Malaysia Shipowners' Association (MASA) highlighted the critical importance of Ship Engineers in

experience, and the necessary credentials or qualifications. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and hire temporary or contract workers. v. The Association of Marine Industries of Malaysia (AMIM) and the Malaysia Shipowners' Association (MASA) highlighted the critical importance of Ship Engineers in ensuring high-quality production in both shipbuilding and repair. These roles are vital for maintaining industry standards and operational efficiency. However, the sector is currently experiencing a significant shortage of skilled Malaysian marine professionals, particularly in engineering positions. This talent gap poses a challenge to meeting the growing demands of the industry and underscores the urgent need to attract and develop more qualified professionals in this field. 193 194

LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT MALAYSIA SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 FIRST QUARTER 2026 Pemakluman Jabatan Perangkaan Malaysia (DOSM) sedang melaksanakan Banci Ekonomi 2026 (BE2026) dengan tema “Data Nadi Ekonomi Rakyat”. Pelaksanaan Banci Ekonomi kali keenam ini berlangsung dari 5 Januari hingga 31 Oktober 2026. BE2026 bertujuan untuk mengumpul data yang menyeluruh dan berstruktur daripada semua pertubuhan perniagaan berdaftar dan tidak berdaftar di Malaysia, bagi menilai prestasi, struktur serta ciri-ciri ekonomi negara secara komprehensif dan berasaskan bukti. Malaysia buat julung kalinya telah menduduki tangga pertama (1) di peringkat global dalam laporan dwi- tahunan Open Data Inventory (ODIN) 2024/25 yang dikeluarkan oleh Open Data Watch (ODW), mengatasi 197 negara lain. Pencapaian ini merupakan lonjakan ketara daripada kedudukan ke-67 dalam penilaian ODIN 2022/23. OpenDOSM NextGen adalah medium yang menyediakan katalog data dan visualisasi bagi memudahkan pengguna menganalisis pelbagai data dan boleh diakses melalui portal https://open.dosm.gov.my. Announcement The Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) is conducting the Economic Census 2026 (BE2026), with themed “Data Nadi Ekonomi Rakyat”. The sixth Economic Census, will be carried out from 5th January to 31st October 2026. BE2026 aims to collect comprehensive and structured data from all registered and unregistered business establishments in Malaysia to assess the nation’s economic performance, structure and characteristics in an evidence-based manner. Malaysia has, for the first time, successfully secured the top position globally in the biennial Open Data Inventory (ODIN) 2024/25 report released by Open Data Watch (ODW), surpassing 197 other countries. This achievement marks a significant leap from its 67th position in the ODIN 2022/23 assessment. OpenDOSM NextGen is a medium that provides data catalogue and visualisations to facilitate users' analysis and can be accessed through https://open.dosm.gov.my. Nota Penerangan Bermula dengan penerbitan Laporan Survei Tenaga Buruh (STB), Suku Tahun Pertama 2026, Malaysia, statistik utama tenaga buruh dianggarkan berdasarkan anggaran penduduk semasa Malaysia. Seiring itu, data STB bagi Suku Tahun Pertama 2024 sehingga Suku Tahun Keempat 2025 turut disemak semula menggunakan data unjuran berdasarkan anggaran penduduk semasa terkini Sementara itu, semakan semula siri data STB Suku Tahun Kedua 2020 hingga Suku Tahun Keempat 2023 masih dilaksanakan. Data tersebut akan diterbitkan selepas proses analisis dan pemukatamadan data selesai dijalankan secara berperingkat. Bagi perincian penjadualan siri data semakan semula, sila layari https://newss. statistics.gov.my/. 8 LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH | SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT | FIRST QUARTER 2026

Explanatory Notes Starting with the release of Labour Force Survey (LFS), First Quarter 2026, Malaysia, the principal statistics of the labour force are estimated based on the current population estimates of Malaysia. On the same note, revisions are carried out on LFS data for the First Quarter of 2024 to the Fourth Quarter of 2025 using projection data based on the latest current population estimates. Meanwhile, the review of the LFS data series for the Second Quarter of 2020 to the Fourth Quarter of 2023 is still ongoing. The data will be published progressively upon the completion of the analysis and finalisation process. 1. Pengenalan Ekonomi Malaysia pada suku tahun pertama 2026 mencatat pertumbuhan Keluaran Dalam Negeri Kasar (KDNK) awalan sebanyak 5.3 peratus, berbanding 6.3 peratus pada suku tahun sebelumnya. Peningkatan ini didorong oleh prestasi yang stabil dalam sektor Perkhidmatan, Pembuatan, Pembinaan dan Pertanian, manakala sektor Perlombongan & pengkuarian mencatatkan penurunan pada suku tahun ini. Dari segi prestasi suku tahun ke suku tahun, ekonomi negara menguncup sebanyak 4.4 peratus berbanding pertumbuhan positif pada suku tahun keempat 2025 sebanyak 3.3 peratus. Memasuki tahun 2026, ekonomi Malaysia menunjukkan tumpuan yang jelas terhadap pengukuhan kesejahteraan rakyat dan rangsangan permintaan domestik melalui pelaksanaan Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (SARA) dan Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR). Kerajaan memperkenalkan SARA Bulanan dengan peruntukan sekitar RM8 bilion kepada 8.1 juta penerima serta bantuan one-off “SARA untuk Semua” kepada kira-kira 22 juta rakyat dewasa, mencerminkan dasar fiskal yang lebih ekspansif dan inklusif dalam menangani kos sara hidup1 Inisiatif ini bukan sahaja meningkatkan kuasa beli rakyat, malah merangsang perbelanjaan pengguna khususnya menjelang musim perayaan, di samping memperkukuh sektor runcit melalui penglibatan lebih banyak premis tempatan. Kadar penggunaan yang tinggi turut menunjukkan keberkesanan penyasaran dasar dalam menyokong permintaan agregat domestik, sekali gus menyumbang kepada kestabilan dan pertumbuhan ekonomi negara secara inklusif. Peningkatan aktiviti ekonomi sempena musim perayaan dan pertumbuhan ekonomi digital terus memperlihatkan impak yang ketara terhadap dinamik pasaran buruh yang dinamik di Malaysia. Penganjuran jualan perayaan dan jualan tengah malam di pusat premium outlets telah merangsang perbelanjaan pengguna serta meningkatkan permintaan tenaga kerja, khususnya dalam sektor runcit dan perkhidmatan2. Selain itu, peningkatan kunjungan ke bazar Aidilfitri seperti di Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman mencerminkan pengukuhan aktiviti ekonomi domestik yang turut menyokong pendapatan peniaga kecil dan pekerja tidak formal3. Dalam masa yang sama, peranan platform digital semakin signifikan apabila TikTok menyumbang sekitar RM20 bilion kepada ekonomi digital negara serta menyokong kira-kira 147,000 peluang pekerjaan, terutamanya dalam kalangan

Aidilfitri seperti di Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman mencerminkan pengukuhan aktiviti ekonomi domestik yang turut menyokong pendapatan peniaga kecil dan pekerja tidak formal3. Dalam masa yang sama, peranan platform digital semakin signifikan apabila TikTok menyumbang sekitar RM20 bilion kepada ekonomi digital negara serta menyokong kira-kira 147,000 peluang pekerjaan, terutamanya dalam kalangan perusahaan mikro, kecil dan sederhana (PMKS)4. Secara analitikal, situasi ini menunjukkan bahawa permintaan bermusim dan pendigitalan ekonomi saling melengkapi dalam merangsang perwujudan jawatan, meningkatkan kadar penyertaan tenaga buruh serta memperluas sumber pendapatan, sekali gus menyokong pertumbuhan ekonomi yang lebih mampan dan inklusif. Tambahan pula, perkembangan pelaburan strategik, reformasi pasaran buruh dan perlindungan sosial pekerja terus memperkukuh struktur ekonomi dan tenaga buruh yang dinamik di Malaysia. Pelaburan bernilai RM30 bilion oleh Intel dalam fasiliti pembungkusan semikonduktor termaju dijangka menjadi pemangkin kepada pembangunan industri bernilai tinggi, sekali gus mewujudkan peluang pekerjaan berkemahiran tinggi serta meningkatkan keupayaan teknologi negara5. Dalam masa yang sama, keperluan memperkukuh hubungan antara gaji dan produktiviti terus diberi penekanan bagi memastikan pertumbuhan pendapatan pekerja selari dengan peningkatan kecekapan dan kemahiran tenaga buruh6. Konsep dan definisi Pelaksanaan Survei Tenaga Buruh di Malaysia adalah berdasarkan garis panduan dan syor International Labour Organization (ILO) dengan merujuk Surveys of Economically Active Population, Employment, Unemployment and Underemployment: An ILO Manual on Concepts and Methods. Paparan 1 : Konsep dan Definisi PENDUDUK UMUR BEKERJA Semua orang yang berumur 15 hingga 64 tahun, sama ada berada di dalam tenaga buruh atau di luar tenaga buruh. TENAGA BURUH Semua orang yang berada dalam kumpulan umur bekerja, sama ada bekerja atau menganggur. PENDUDUK BEKERJA Semua orang yang bekerja sekurang-kurangnya sejam semasa minggu rujukan untuk mendapatkan upah, keuntungan atau keuntungan keluarga sama ada sebagai majikan, pekerja, bekerja sendiri atau pekerja keluarga tanpa gaji. PENGANGGUR Semua orang yang tidak bekerja semasa minggu rujukan dan dikelaskan kepada dua kumpulan iaitu penganggur aktif dan penganggur tidak aktif. LUAR TENAGA BURUH Semua orang yang tidak dikelaskan sebagai bekerja atau menganggur dikategorikan sebagai luar tenaga buruh. Kategori ni terdiri daripada suri rumah, pelajar (termasuk mereka yang akan melanjutkan pelajaran), mereka yang telah bersara, tidak berkeupayaan dan tidak berminat untuk mencari pekerjaan. 3. Dari segi tahun ke tahun, jumlah penduduk bekerja meningkat sebanyak 1.0 peratus, atau bersamaan peningkatan 161.5 ribu orang berbanding suku tahun pertama 2025 (ST1 2025r: 16.56 juta orang). Lelaki merangkumi 62.6 peratus daripada jumlah guna tenaga dengan 10.47 juta orang, meningkat sebanyak 0.2 peratus berbanding suku tahun sebelumnya. Sementara itu, wanita mewakili 37.4 peratus daripada jumlah guna tenaga, meningkat 0.4 peratus untuk merekodkan 6.25 juta orang. Dari segi kumpulan umur, penduduk bekerja kebanyakannya dalam kumpulan umur 25 hingga 34 tahun, iaitu merangkumi 30.6 peratus (5.11 juta orang). Kumpulan ini diikuti oleh mereka yang berumur 35 hingga 44 tahun pada 27.5 peratus (4.61 juta orang) dan kumpulan umur 45 hingga 54 tahun sebanyak 18.1 peratus (3.02 juta orang). 1. Introduction Malaysia’s economy in the first quarter of 2026, recorded an advance Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth of 5.3 per cent, compared to 6.3 per cent in the preceding quarter. This growth was driven by stable performance in the Services, Manufacturing, Construction, and Agriculture sectors, while the Mining and Quarrying sector recorded a decline during the quarter. On a quarter-on-quarter basis, the national economy contracted significantly by 4.4 per cent, compared to a positive growth of 3.3 per cent in the fourth quarter of 2025. Entering 2026, Malaysia’s economy demonstrates a clear focus on strengthening public well-being and stimulating domestic demand through the implementation of Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (SARA) and Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR). The Government introduced the monthly SARA programme with an allocation of approximately RM8 billion benefiting 8.1 million recipients, alongside a one-off “SARA untuk Semua” assistance of RM100 for about 22 million adult Malaysians, reflecting a more expansionary and inclusive fiscal policy in addressing the cost of living1. These initiatives not only enhance households’ purchasing power but also stimulate consumer spending, particularly ahead of festive seasons, while reinforcing the retail sector through greater participation of local outlets. The high utilisation rate of these assistance programmes further indicates effective policy targeting in supporting domestic aggregate demand, thereby contributing to overall economic stability and inclusive growth. The increase in economic activities during festive seasons, alongside the continued expansion of the digital economy, has demonstrated a significant impact on the dynamics of Malaysia’s labour market. The organisation of festive sales and midnight sales at premium outlet centres has stimulated consumer spending and increased labour demand, particularly in the retail and services sectors2. Furthermore, the rise in visitor traffic to Aidilfitri bazaars, such as those in Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman, reflects the strengthening of domestic economic activity, which also supports

sales and midnight sales at premium outlet centres has stimulated consumer spending and increased labour demand, particularly in the retail and services sectors2. Furthermore, the rise in visitor traffic to Aidilfitri bazaars, such as those in Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman, reflects the strengthening of domestic economic activity, which also supports the income of small traders and informal workers3. At the same time, the role of digital platforms has become increasingly significant, with TikTok contributing approximately RM20 billion to the national digital economy and supporting around 147,000 job opportunities, particularly among micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs)4. From an analytical perspective, this situation indicates that seasonal demand and economic digitalisation complement each other in driving job creation, increasing labour force participation, and diversifying income sources, thereby supporting more sustainable and inclusive economic growth. Notably, the advancement of strategic investments, labour market reforms, and worker social protection continues to strengthen Malaysia’s economic structure and labour market dynamics. The RM30 billion investment by Intel in an advanced semiconductor packaging facility is expected to serve as a catalyst for the development of high-value industries, while creating high skilled employment opportunities and enhancing the nation’s technological capabilities5. At the same time, greater emphasis is being placed on strengthening the linkage between wages and productivity to ensure that income growth is aligned with improvements in labour efficiency and skills6. In addition, the proposed implementation of mandatory contributions for gig workers is viewed as an important step in expanding social protection and safeguarding the long-term economic wellbeing of this group7. intensive and high value-added sectors, alongside efforts to reinforce labour market institutions through improvements in compensation systems and social protection, thereby supporting more inclusive, resilient, and sustainable economic growth. The Labour Force Survey Report for the first quarter of 2026 presents statistics of the labour force based on the Labour Force Survey conducted by the Department of Statistics Malaysia. The survey is carried out using probability sampling through a household approach to collect data on the structure of the labour force, employment, and unemployment, covering Malaysian citizens and non-citizens. The principal statistics are presented according to selected demographic and socioeconomic characteristics such as sex, age group, educational attainment, status in employment and occupation. The report elaborates on the quarter on-quarter changes to examine the immediate effect of socioeconomic events that occurred throughout the quarter. Users are advised to interpret these short-term changes with caution since the data are not seasonally adjusted. In addition, annual changes from the same quarter of the preceding year are also reported. 2. Concepts and definitions The implementation of Labour Force Survey in Malaysia is based on the guidelines and recommendations of the International Labour Organization (ILO) with reference to the Surveys of Economically Active Population, Employment, Unemployment and Underemployment: An ILO Manual on Concepts and Methods. Exhibit 1: Concepts and Definitions WORKING AGE POPULATION All persons aged between 15 to 64 years who are either in the labour force or outside the labour force. LABOUR FORCE All persons in the working age who are either employed or unemployed. EMPLOYED All persons who at any time during the reference week worked at least one hour for pay, profit or family gain either as employers, employees, own-account workers or unpaid family workers. UNEMPLOYED All persons who did not work during the reference week and are classified into

employed or unemployed. EMPLOYED All persons who at any time during the reference week worked at least one hour for pay, profit or family gain either as employers, employees, own-account workers or unpaid family workers. UNEMPLOYED All persons who did not work during the reference week and are classified into two groups that are actively unemployed and inactively unemployed. OUTSIDE LABOUR FORCE All persons not classified as employed or unemployed are classified as outside labour force. This category consists of housewives, students (including those going for further studies), retired, disabled person and those not interested in looking for jobs. 28 LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH | SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT | FIRST QUARTER 2026

3. Labour force The labour force participation rate remained unchanged at 70.9 per cent The labour force expanded by 0.3 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, adding 46.6 thousand persons to reach a total of 17.23 million persons (Q4 2025r: 17.19 million persons). The Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR) stood at 70.9 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, maintaining the same level observed in the preceding quarter. The employment-to-population ratio, a key measure of an economy's capacity for job creation, held steady at 68.8 per cent. During the same period, the number of outside labour force grew by 17.5 thousand persons, reaching a total of 7.08 million persons (Q4 2025r: 7.06 million persons) as shown in Exhibit 2. Compared to the first quarter of the previous year, the labour force rose by 145.4 thousand persons (Q1 2025r: 17.09 million persons). Additionally, the LFPR saw an increase of 0.1 percentage points (Q1 2025r: 70.8%). 4. Employed persons The number of employed persons continued to expand, recording a growth of 0.3 per cent The number of employed persons continued to register a positive growth in the first quarter of 2026, increasing by 0.3 per cent (+48.5 thousand persons) to 16.73 million persons (Q4 2025r: 16.68 million persons). On a year-on-year comparison, total employment edged up by 1.0 per cent, or equivalent to an increase of 161.5 thousand persons compared to the first quarter of 2025 (Q1 2025r: 16.56 million persons). Males comprised 62.6 per cent of the employed persons with 10.47 million persons, rose up by 0.2 per cent compared to the previous quarter. Meanwhile, females represented 37.4 per cent of the employed persons, an increase of 0.4 per cent to record 6.25 million persons. In terms of age group, the majority of employed persons were in the 25 to 34 years age group, accounting for 30.6 per cent (5.11 million persons). This was followed by those aged 35 to 44 at 27.5 per cent (4.61 million persons), and those aged 45 to 54 at 18.1 per cent (3.02 million persons). According to educational attainment, employed persons with secondary education represented 56.6 per cent of total employment with 9.47 million persons (Q4 2025r: 56.6%; 9.45 million persons). Meanwhile, employed persons with tertiary education were the second-largest category at 32.8 per cent or 5.49 million persons (Q4 2025r: 32.8%; 5.47 million persons), as shown in Exhibit 5. Unemployed and unemployment rate Unemployment rate dropped by 0.1 percentage points to 2.9 per cent The number of unemployed persons edged down by 0.4 per cent during the first quarter of 2026, reaching 506.5 thousand compared to 508.3 thousand persons in the previous quarter. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate decreased by 0.1 percentage point to 2.9 per cent. Year-on-year comparison showed the number of unemployed persons fell by 3.1 per cent (-16.1 thousand persons) compared to first quarter of 2025 (Q1 2025r: -29.0 thousand persons), leading to a 2.9 per cent unemployment rate, an improvement from the 3.1 per cent seen in the previous year. In the first quarter of 2026, the number of unemployed youths increased by 0.6 per cent to 279.9 thousand persons (Q4 2025r: 278.2 thousand persons), while the number of unemployed adults decreased by 3.6 thousand persons to 226.5 thousand persons (Q4 2025r: 230.1 thousand persons). The unemployment rates for youths aged 15 to 24 years and adults aged 25 to 64 years remained unchanged at 9.8 per cent and 1.6 per cent, respectively in the first quarter of 2026. A year-on-year comparison showed that the unemployed youth increased by 4.3 thousand persons, while the youth unemployment rate remained unchanged as in the first quarter of 2025. On the other hand, the number of unemployed adults reduced by of 20.4 thousand persons, with a 0.1 percentage points declined for the adult unemployment rate (Q1 2025r: 246.9 thousand persons; 1.7%) as depicted in Chart 9. On an industry level, as shown in Figure 1.4, sectors with a high degree of dependence on global supply chains, such as Automotive and Aerospace (46%), and Mining and Metals (55%), expect industry transformation driven by trade restrictions. By contrast, industries with less exposure to global markets, such as Education, are less focused on this trend, with fewer than 14% of surveyed employers seeing trade restrictions as disruptive. Mining and Metals, Advanced Manufacturing, and Oil and Gas anticipate industry transformation stemming from increased government subsidies and industrial policies, with, respectively, 31%, 33%, and 40% of employers across these industries citing these factors; whereas more domestic-focused sectors such as Accommodation, Food, and Leisure expect minimal impact from such policies. The broader implications of geoeconomic fragmentation extend beyond individual business strategies to long-term economic stability and growth, and limit multilateral cooperation on critical issues such as climate change and pandemic preparedness.24 Future of Jobs Report 2025 14

Introduction About the study Ready to navigate the dynamic landscape of Malaysia’s job market in 2025? We are proud to share with you the latest edition of our Hiring, Compensation and Benefits report. Part of our Signature Report series (ongoing since 2022), this report delves into the outlook of the hiring market, hiring activity and plans, compensation and benefits offered by companies, with a focus on AI in hiring and workplace diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI). With data gathered from 2,279 hirers and HR professionals across a diverse range of industries and job roles in Malaysia, the Hiring, Compensation and Benefits 2025 report for the Malaysian market reflects SEEK’s commitment to your success. More than just a collection of facts and figures, it's a roadmap to attract top-tier talent and nurture their growth in a workplace culture that aligns seamlessly with the Malaysian work environment. We present this to you as part of our ongoing commitment as your No.1 Trusted Talent Partner in Asia. These results provide deep actionable insights for your market, enabling you to better connect talent to #BetterMatches. Key findings from this report include: Hiring outlook in the market Improvements in compensation offered by other companies in the market Benefits offered by companies to attract and retain highly-skilled talent General perceptions of AI in hiring and DEI initiatives Disclaimer: In some occurrences in this report, it is possible that percentages do not add up to or exceed 100% as a result of rounding of numeral fractions. Introduction Foreword Aimee Hutton Head of APAC Marketing Strategy To say that 2024 was a year of significant change is an understatement. Companies and talent alike are now navigating a world where technological advancements, economic shifts and societal values are reshaping expectations and possibilities. At SEEK, we are proud to be at the forefront of this evolution, constantly innovating to meet the dynamic needs of employers and job seekers in Malaysia and beyond. Everything we do at SEEK adopts a customer-first mindset. We understand that employers and talent have preferences and needs that are ever-changing. Whether it's a shift in hiring trends, the growing focus on compensation and benefits, or the increasing importance of diversity in the workplace, we strive to stay one step ahead so that you're ahead of the curve. Our purpose has never wavered: to help people live more fulfilling and productive working lives, and help organisations succeed. To serve this purpose, we completed a three-year product-unification programme that brought together our Jobstreet, Jobsdb and SEEK platforms under one cohesive framework. This monumental effort was designed to provide greater access to talent and job opportunities, improve search and match relevance, and enhance the overall user experience. With this unified platform, we can now innovate faster, roll out new products and features more efficiently across the region, and create unparalleled value for our users. Whether you're an employer looking for the right talent or a professional seeking your next career move, our platform offers the tools and support to make that connection seamless and meaningful. This report digs deep into core factors that affect the workforce's motivations - namely Hiring Trends, Compensation and Benefits. You'll discover some of the most common full-time functions hired over the past year, the key reasons for shifting headcounts, as well as the

to make that connection seamless and meaningful. This report digs deep into core factors that affect the workforce's motivations - namely Hiring Trends, Compensation and Benefits. You'll discover some of the most common full-time functions hired over the past year, the key reasons for shifting headcounts, as well as the average performance bonuses and salary increments given out in 2024. The report also sheds light on how flexibility, well-being, and fairness are becoming core pillars of competitive workplace offerings. 02

Introduction Foreword Another critical theme explored in this report is the growing emphasis on diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) in the workplace. In Malaysia and across the region, businesses are recognising that fostering a diverse and equitable workplace is not only a moral imperative but also a strategic advantage. By embracing DEI, companies can tap into a wider range of perspectives, ideas and talents – driving innovation and long-term success. Some of the key trends highlighted in the report: Hiring plans for H1 2025: It is forecasted that Malaysia’s 2025 GDP growth will be at 4.6% and close to half expressed plans to expand their permanent workforce in the first half of 2025. AI’s increasing influence: 70% of businesses consider candidates’ AI knowledge during hiring, while 36% of companies see AI knowledge as highly important. Diversity and inclusivity needs more prioritisation: Malaysia’s DEI still has room for improvement, as only 2 out of 5 companies adopt DEI initiatives, while 25% will not adopt any DEI initiative in the next 12 months. As we navigate these exciting changes, SEEK remains committed to its ambition: to help 500 million people develop their careers and connect five million companies with the right talent. The findings presented in this report are a testament to our dedication to understanding the needs of our customers and delivering solutions that make a meaningful impact. We hope these insights will empower you to navigate the challenges and opportunities of the modern workplace with confidence. We’ll continue to see you into the new year with SEEK as your partner in this journey. Together, we are shaping the future of work, connecting the right people to the right job opportunities. Aimee Hutton Head of APAC Marketing Strategy, SEEK 03

Section 1 Hiring trends: today and tomorrow Hiring the best is your most important task. -Steve Jobs The first section of the report examines both past and future hiring trends, offering valuable insights into the evolving job market. These insights can help employers refine their recruitment strategies and empower job seekers to better prepare for opportunities in the year ahead. This section addresses the following questions: 1 How active is the local hiring market and what is the outlook for the next year? 2 What are the hiring activities in 2024, and what types of employees and job functions were hired? 3 What are the workforce reducing activities in 2024, and which types of employees and job functions were let go? 4 What are the future hiring plans for permanent employees? 5 What are the future hiring plans for contract or temporary employees?

Hiring Trends: Today & Tomorrow Employment market expectations Despite much economic uncertainty globally and in Malaysia in 2024, the Malaysian job market has demonstrated resilience. 74% of respondents believe that the Malaysian job market is active in 2024. Confidence remains high for 2025. 39% is confident that the job market will be more active, while 44% think it will be as active in the first half of the year as it is today – reflecting a strongly optimistic outlook of the marketplace. However, 9% are uncertain about the outlook in the second half of 2025 - which could be influenced by various factors such as economic conditions and global events. Hiring Trends: Today & Tomorrow Overview of hiring activity in 2024 According to the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM), employment grew by over 203,000 across Malaysia as of August 2024. The statistic is consistent with our survey where nearly all surveyed companies hired at least one employee during the year, maintaining a strong hiring trend from 2023 at 94%. Permanent full-time employees continue to account for the majority of hires, accounting for 85% of the new hires. However, the hiring landscape and the types of employment are still changing, with companies opting for more temporary support instead of full-time headcount. The biggest change continues to come from the hiring of part-time, contractual and temporary employees, which increased by approximately 8 to 10 percentage points compared to 2023. Hiring Trends: Today & Tomorrow Overview of hiring activity in 2024 Looking at hiring levels from a broader perspective, more small businesses hired in 2024, which increased to 93% from 88% in the previous year. The growth was primarily driven by an increase in hiring part-time, contractual, and temporary employees. Large and medium-sized businesses have mainly maintained their hiring rate from the previous year. While these businesses are hiring more part-time, contractual, and temporary employees, this growth was offset by a decline in the hiring of permanent full-time employees. Hiring Trends: Today & Tomorrow Overview of hiring activity in 2024 For the 94% of companies that hired in 2024, the top 4 job functions remained largely unchanged from 2023. Admin and HR held the top spot at 41%, followed by Accounting (33%) and Sales / Business Development at 21%. The new entry in the top 5 permanent full-time job functions hired was Management roles, also at 21%. Hiring Trends: Today & Tomorrow Overview of hiring activity in 2024 Despite strong hiring trends, many companies also reduced the size of their workforce. Workforce reduction in this context refers to either layoffs, retrenchments, or not replacing employees after they resign and leave the organisation. In 2024, 39% of companies reduced the number of employees hired. Permanent full-time employees accounted for the largest share of those that were let go, at 24%, followed closely by part-time, contractual and temporary employees. Despite these reductions, it is likely that many of these affected employees found roles in other companies due to the strong hiring trend. National statistics indicated that employment continued to grow, and the unemployment rate declined from 3.4% to 3.2%. Hiring Trends: Today & Tomorrow Permanent employees hiring plans for H1 2025 The Asian Development Bank (ADB) forecasted that Malaysia’s GDP growth in 2025 will be at 4.6%. Reflecting this sentiment, nearly half of the businesses surveyed plan to expand their permanent workforce in the first half of 2025. The primary reasons for many of them are i) expanding their business (83%), ii) requiring new roles / skillsets (58%), and iii) replacing staff who have resigned / been laid-off / let go (43%). About 46% of companies surveyed said that while they are not pursuing workforce expansion, they will opt for workforce stability by maintaining or freezing headcount. Around 5%, primarily large businesses are planning to reduce headcount, citing reasons such as reducing operating costs, restructuring / changing employee functions, and anticipating potential economic challenges ahead. Section 2 Compensation and Increments I have lived on this earth long enough to conclude that the only time the average man is truly happy is during salary day. Unknown Author In a dynamic job market, employee wages and inflation are closely linked. Wages heavily influence employment decisions, while inflation guides compensation strategies and salary increment estimates. Offering competitive compensation is essential for attracting and retaining top talent. This section of our report examines the following questions: 1 What initiatives did businesses take to evaluate and enhance compensation and benefits? 2 How many companies awarded performance bonuses, and what were the payouts? 3 How many companies provided salary increments, and what were the increments? 4 How many staff promotions were granted, and what were the increments?

Compensation and Increments Initiatives to evaluate and enhance compensation and benefits Employees will be pleased to learn that many businesses stepped up their efforts to evaluate their compensation and benefits! More businesses have taken initiatives to ensure competitive salary packages by evaluating pay guidelines, factoring in inflation, and revising structures. They are also benchmarking their benefits to ensure that they have competitive benefits to attract and retain talent. Encouragingly, more businesses are listening to employee feedback by conducting employee engagement surveys to gauge employee sentiment, though there is still significant room for improvement. Compensation and Increments Initiatives to evaluate and enhance compensation and benefits Most of the improvements in compensation and benefits evaluation initiatives come from small businesses, and they are catching up with their medium and larger peers, perhaps likely in a bid to attract top talent. Medium-sized businesses saw improvements in factoring inflation and revising pay structures, while large businesses focused more on benchmarking benefits. Compensation and Increments Performance bonuses in 2024 2024 was a positive year for employees, as over three-quarters of companies provided bonuses in various forms. Performance bonuses remain the most popular type of bonus given. The size of the average bonus payout increased to approximately 2 months' salary, slightly higher than in the previous year. More companies are also being transparent regarding employee compensation. 56% of businesses shared the method used to calculate bonus payouts with their employees, compared to 46% in the previous year. Increased openness and transparency can help foster positive employer-employee relationships. Compensation and Increments Annual salary increases in 2024 The post-Covid recovery in 2022 was a great year for businesses, leading to more business giving out higher annual increments in 2023. However, the ‘normalisation’ and challenging business conditions of 2023 may result in annual increments returning to ‘normal’. In 2024, the number of companies that gave annual increments to employees declined slightly to 77%, compared to 85% a year ago. Most of the increments given were between 1% and 5%. Encouragingly, these increments have kept up with the national annual inflation rate of 2.49% in 2023. Section 3 Leave and General Benefits Happy employees ensure happy customers. And happy customers ensure happy shareholders, in that order. -Simon Sinek Part 3 of the report focuses on leave and general benefits. Simon Sinek stresses that employee satisfaction is crucial for business success, noting that how management treats employees directly influences organisational performance, shaping outcomes for better or worse. Therefore, it is crucial for businesses to continuously evolve their benefits to support employees and keep them motivated. This section of our report addresses the following questions: 1 What types of benefits do companies currently offer? 2 What types of benefits are trending (i.e. benefits that companies offered in 2024 or plan to offer in the next 12 months)? Special leave Financial benefits Career development programmes Accommodation benefits Work-life balance benefits Family-friendly benefits Others

Leave & General Benefits Current and trending Special Leaves Compassionate leave, marriage leave, and replacement leave (newly added in our survey) are the top 3 types of special leaves offered by businesses, making them almost essential for attracting talent. In 2024, the benefits that continued to gain popularity are additional paternity and maternity leaves, alongside replacement leave—each at 16% respectively. This trend may indicate businesses’ intent to better support their employees’ family life. Section 4 AI in the Hiring Workplace There was a time after the iPhone App Store launch where people talked about being a mobile company. But no software company says they’re a mobile company now because it’d be unthinkable to not have a mobile app. And it’ll be unthinkable not to have intelligence integrated into every product and service. -Sam Altman, co-founder and CEO, Open AI With AI dominating headlines over the past year, part 4 of our report focuses on AI in the hiring workplace. It examines whether businesses place importance on AI knowledge among candidates, or whether they leverage AI tools to help with the hiring process. This section of our report looks into the following questions: 1 Is it important for candidates to have AI knowledge and how are they assessed? 2 Do businesses use AI tools for recruitment and what do they use it for? 3 What are the reasons businesses choose to adopt and reject AI tools?

AI in the Hiring Workplace Importance of AI knowledge among candidates With the rise of AI in recent years, 70% of businesses now consider candidates’ AI knowledge when hiring. Of these, 36% view AI knowledge as highly important, considering it either a primary consideration or as important as other qualifications. These businesses primarily assess AI knowledge via candidate self-introduction, asking technical questions, or reviewing projects or work samples in the candidate’s portfolio. Importance of candidates having AI knowledge 13% AI knowledge is a primary consideration in hiring decisions 30% AI knowledge is given equal importance alongside other qualifications 23% AI knowledge is considered, but not as important as other qualifications AI knowledge is not a significant factor in hiring decisions 34% Assessment of AI knowledge Based on candidates’s self-introduction 51% during interview Technical interview questions 50% specifically related to AI concepts Reviewing AI-related projects or 46% work samples in the candidate’s portfolio Reference checks with previous 40% AI-related work experience Certifications or academic 40% qualifications in AI AI-specific coding or 36% problem-solving assessments 33

AI in the Hiring Workplace Usage of AI tool for recruitment Given the importance of AI knowledge, it is no surprise that some companies are also using AI to recruit suitable candidates. 26% of companies mentioned using AI tools for recruitment, primarily for tasks like job ad writing, where AI helps with language editing, generating comprehensive qualifications, and making it more professional. More than half of those using AI tools also use it for candidate screening and assessment, which may include generating important questions, conducting personality assessments, creating scenarios, and so on. 26% have adopted AI tools to assist with recruitment 71% 59% 54% 44% 44% 35% Job ad Candidate Candidate Interview Background Onboarding writing screening assessment process checks process 34
