Section 5 Diversity, Equity and Inclusion (DEI) at the workplace If it’s truly mission critical, if giving our employees the opportunity to express themselves, to be part of something, to make change, to lead the grassroots movement, then it has to be in the same category as everything else that we do that is mission critical. -Jodie Wallis, Global Chief Analytics Officer, Manulife Catalyst Honours, Toronto (7 Oct 2024) The implementation of DEI in the workplace is still in its infancy in many Asian countries. The final part of this report explores DEI and provides ideas that we hope will encourage greater adoption by companies. This section addresses the following questions: 1 What percentage of businesses currently adopt DEI measures or initiatives? 2 What DEI measures or initiatives are being adopted? 3 What percentage are planning to adopt DEI measures or initiatives? 4 What preparation work has been done to adopt DEI measures or initiatives? 5 What percentage are not adopting DEI measures or initiatives? 6 What are the factors contributing to hesitancy or reluctance? 7 What types of fair hiring measures do companies employ?

Diversity, Equity and Inclusion (DEI) at the workplace DEI adoption and initiatives Malaysia is a melting pot of diversity. Thus, it is important to place diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) at the heart of the workplace to foster an environment where every voice is heard. DEI refers to initiatives or policies designed to make people from various backgrounds feel welcomed and supported, allowing them to perform to their fullest potential. Examples of diversity include culture, ethnicity, gender, age, and more. Currently, only 2 out of 5 companies have adopted DEI initiatives, while 25% of businesses do not plan to implement any DEI initiative in the next 12 months. Encouragingly, 2% plan to implement DEI, while 33% are considering to do so in the next 12 months. of organisation currently adopt 40% initiatives to promote workplace diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI). 2% 33% will adopt and may adopt initiatives to promote workplace diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) in the next 12 months. will not adopt initiatives to promote 25% workplace diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) in the next 12 months. 3371

Diversity, Equity and Inclusion (DEI) at the workplace Fair hiring measures or initiatives When it comes to fair hiring initiatives, the most common measures companies have implemented include clear diversity and inclusion goals for the hiring process, having diverse hiring panels to ensure a range of perspectives (e.g. HR partners, senior managers, employees from other departments), and conducting blind resume screenings to eliminate bias in the initial stages by removing criteria such as names, gender, age, and so on. Efforts to ensure job descriptions are free from bias includes regularly reviewing and updating job descriptions, and using standardised templates across roles. Conclusion & Recommendations Key Insights: Malaysia Strong 2024 and optimistic 2025 job market outlook In 2024, Malaysia’s job market remained resilient despite global and national economic uncertainties, with 74% of respondents indicating the job marketplace as active. Employment grew by over 200,000 jobs, and 94% of companies hired, primarily for permanent, full-time roles. Part-time and temporary positions also saw notable growth, especially within small businesses. Despite some companies reducing their workforce, the overall unemployment rate remained low, dropping to 3.2%, indicating that those affected were able to find new employment quickly. Confidence for 2025 remains high, with 39% of businesses expecting growth and 44% anticipating stability in the first half of the year. Some businesses plan to expand their permanent workforce, though some, especially large companies, are considering headcount freezes or reductions due to cost concerns. Stronger compensation, with a return to ‘normal’ compensation In 2024, many Malaysian businesses made efforts to improve compensation and benefits to attract and retain talent. Companies, particularly small businesses, reevaluated salary packages, adjusted for inflation, and benchmarked benefits, showing a commitment to competitive compensation. More than 75% of companies provided bonuses, with average payouts slightly higher than the previous year. Increased transparency in bonus calculations promoted positive employer-employee relationships. While 77% of companies offered annual increments (mostly 1% to 5%), slightly lower than the previous year. However, these adjustments matched the national inflation rate, ensuring real wage growth for employees. Promotions remained stable at 58%, but those promoted saw higher pay increases, with the number of companies offering over 10% increments nearly doubling. These measures reflect a positive employment climate, encouraging career growth and loyalty among employees. 42
number of companies offering over 10% increments nearly doubling. These measures reflect a positive employment climate, encouraging career growth and loyalty among employees. 42

Conclusion & Recommendations Key Insights: Malaysia More benefits continuing trends from 2024 In 2024, Malaysian businesses increasingly provide special leaves such as compassionate, marriage, and replacement leave, making them nearly standard offerings to attract talent. Paternity and maternity leave are also on the rise, reflecting a growing commitment to supporting families. Mandatory social benefits, medical insurance, and health screenings top the list of financial benefits, with a focus on employee health and protection. Accommodation benefits are typically limited to industries reliant on foreign labour, like manufacturing and construction. Companies are prioritising upskilling through training, self-learning programmes, and job rotations. In-office perks such as free snacks, casual dress codes, and early leave on festive days support work-life balance, complement the flexible work arrangements introduced post-Covid 19. Traditional office hours are shifting, with more employees working varied hours instead of the classic 9-to-6. Although still rare, family-friendly benefits include events for family members, family health insurance, and nursing rooms. Transportation allowances and free parking are common, and are considered essential for encouraging office attendance. Moving forward, companies plan to enhance connectivity through work phone packages, signaling a commitment to supporting both work flexibility and in-office productivity. 43

Conclusion & Recommendations Key Insights: Malaysia AI knowledge is gaining importance in hiring With the rise of AI, 70% of businesses now consider candidates' AI knowledge during hiring, with 36% viewing it as a primary or essential qualification. Companies assess this knowledge through self-introductions, technical questions, and portfolio reviews. Additionally, 26% of businesses are using AI in recruitment processes, particularly for job ad writing, candidate screening, and assessments, which enhances efficiency and job matching. However, some businesses avoid AI tools due to a perceived lack of personalisation, limited access or knowledge, and concerns about costs. Two out of five companies practiced DEI amid challenges In Malaysia’s diverse workforce, DEI (Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) is key to a thriving workplace. Currently, only 40% of companies have implemented DEI, but 35% are planning to or may consider adopting DEI measures in 2025. These 40% of companies have adopted top DEI initiatives such as anti-discrimination policies, unconscious bias training, and employee resource groups, measuring success through employee satisfaction and diversity metrics. However, 25% of businesses aren’t planning DEI initiatives, citing a lack of legal mandates, unclear ROI, and limited management focus. Common fair hiring practices include setting diversity goals, using diverse hiring panels, and blind resume screening. 44

Conclusion & Recommendations Recommendations As of the writing of this report, Malaysia's Budget 2025 has been recently unveiled. Businesses and hirers are encouraged to review its details when planning for 2025. 1. Seize the opportunity to recruit top talent in 2025 Given the strong confidence in the job market in H1, businesses should continue seeking talent to bolster their workforce. Businesses that were previously focused on hiring only full-time employees might consider exploring part-time, contractual, or temporary employees. As highlighted in this report, a flexible workforce offers numerous benefits. 2. Offer competitive increments to motivate employees SEEK recently unveiled Talent Attraction Lab, a separate study that focuses on employee motivations across industries and demographics. The study reveals that compensation is the top driver for attracting and retaining employees. It is critical for businesses to factor in inflation, industry benchmarks, and company performance to determine competitive salary increments. 3. Enhance benefits packages by introducing popular offerings Employee benefits continue to evolve, and while many remained the same as last year, new and emerging options are gaining popularity. To attract and retain top talent, consider offering the most sought-after benefits highlighted in the leave and general benefits section of this report. 4. Prioritise talent with AI knowledge in the right industries Insights from SEEK’s 'Decoding Global Talent 2024: Shifting work preferences in the Age of AI' reveal that the adoption of AI amongst talent is substantial, with 44% of talent already using AI tools monthly and it rightly matches business expectations. Hence, hirers should look for talent with AI knowledge and equip themselves with AI tools for recruitment. Businesses can also leverage various initiatives under Malaysia’s National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Roadmap 2021-2025 and Human Resource Development Corporation (HRD Corp) to improve their workforce. 5. Begin implementing Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) policies

hirers should look for talent with AI knowledge and equip themselves with AI tools for recruitment. Businesses can also leverage various initiatives under Malaysia’s National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Roadmap 2021-2025 and Human Resource Development Corporation (HRD Corp) to improve their workforce. 5. Begin implementing Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) policies Malaysia’s diverse cultural landscape makes it crucial for companies to provide safe environments and equal opportunities for all. There are many advantages to having a strong DEI policy, such as fostering a strong diverse workforce, building values that resonate with customers, and enhancing your company's reputation. Additionally, Malaysia’s Budget 2025 includes tax incentives for measures like additional paid leave for caring for children or ill / disable family members or hiring women returning to the workforce.
